---
title: "5.2 Edge cases and failure modes"
---

## Explanation

An edge case is an input near a boundary of the specification. A failure mode is a way the code or method can give a wrong, misleading, or unusable result.

For example, an average function might fail for:

- an empty array,
- integer overflow in a low-level language,
- missing values,
- a user expecting weighted average instead of ordinary average.

Some failures are programming failures. Others are scientific failures: the code runs, but the result does not support the claim.

## Things to look up

- Edge case
- Failure mode
- Boundary condition
- Invalid input
- Defensive programming

## Exercise

For each task, list at least three edge cases and two failure modes:

- computing an average,
- finding a root of a function,
- running a Monte Carlo simulation.

## Notes for the exercise

- Include both programming failures and scientific failures.
- Do not list only syntax errors.
- Include cases where the code runs but the result is misleading.
